EXP 0.1

0.1 — baseline harness smoke run

Proof the pieces wire together: two transformer baselines, amortized loss-per-FLOP.

status: harness validation

This is the proof the pieces wire together end-to-end — not a claim about enwik8. The corpus is the tiny bundled English sample (~5 KB), so absolute bpb is not comparable to the literature; only the shape of the curves and the harness mechanics matter.

Why this run exists

Task 0.1 builds the foundation: a byte-level data pipeline, an honest FLOP counter, a transformer baseline behind a plug-in model interface, a train loop that stops at a fixed training-FLOP budget, and a leaderboard. This run proves the metric is wired correctly.

Setup

  • Corpus: bundled offline sample, deterministic 90/10 train/val split.
  • Two transformer baselines (pre-norm, RMSNorm, RoPE, causal), same budget 5×10105\times10^{10}5×1010 training FLOPs (a ceiling — both stop at ≤\le≤ budget), same fixed eval context (64 bytes), CPU, seed 0:
    • d32 — d_model=32, n_layers=2 → 32,928 params, 220 steps.
    • d64 — d_model=64, n_layers=3 → 164,288 params, 46 steps.
  • Both start from the uninformed baseline of 8.0 bpb (uniform over 256 bytes).

Result

modelparamssteps @ 5×10105\times10^{10}5×1010 FLOPsfinal val bpb
d3232,9282203.37
d64164,288463.82

What the plot shows

The instructive bit: at equal FLOPs the smaller model (d32) is lower (3.37 vs 3.82). Each d32 step is far cheaper, so the same budget buys ∼\sim∼5× more optimizer steps, and on a tiny corpus that extra optimization wins over the bigger model’s per-step capacity.

the lesson

This is exactly why the metric is per FLOP and read off a curve, not from a single model at a single size: “bigger” is not automatically “better per FLOP,” and a one-budget snapshot can mislead. (See loss-per-FLOP & scaling laws.)

read honestly

Visualize honestly: this is a 5 KB sample, so the bigger model is also data-starved here. The lesson is methodological (how to read bpb-vs-FLOP curves), not a finding about transformer scaling.

Reproduce

uv run smolml train --data sample --d-model 32 --layers 2 --seq-len 64 \
    --eval-seq-len 64 --budget 5e10 --run-name baseline-d32
uv run smolml train --data sample --d-model 64 --layers 3 --seq-len 64 \
    --eval-seq-len 64 --budget 5e10 --run-name baseline-d64
uv run smolml leaderboard --runs-dir runs

See also

  • Loss-per-FLOP & scaling laws — the “lower curve wins” idea.
  • 0.2 — prequential baseline — the same harness under the real metric.
